name: Coherent Joint Transmission (CJT) Type II Codebook

lineage.adds: a per-transmission-point beam report plus an inter-site phase alignment, so several base stations can transmit as one distributed array

tagline: Several separated base-station sites acting as one giant antenna array — describe each site's beam, then say how to phase-align the sites so their signals add up instead of cancel.
overview: Ordinary Type II describes a precoder for one base station's antenna array. **Coherent joint transmission (CJT)** asks a harder question: what if **several geographically separated transmission points (TRPs)** — think several nearby cell sites — want to serve one phone jointly, as if they were a single giant distributed array? For that to work coherently (signals adding constructively rather than randomly canceling), the phone (**UE**) must report not just each site's beam, but the **relative phase and timing offset between sites**.

The CJT codebook is, structurally, eType II R16 **replicated once per cooperating TRP**, glued together with two genuinely new ingredients. First, each TRP $j$ gets its **own** spatial report — its own orthogonal beam group and its own $L_j$ selected DFT (discrete Fourier transform) beams, since different sites see the channel from different angles. Second, an **inter-TRP co-phasing** term $\psi_j$ — a per-TRP frequency-dependent phase ramp — corrects for the relative delay between sites, exactly the quantity needed for their signals to combine coherently rather than destructively at the phone. Everything else — the delay-tap compression, the amplitude/phase quantization tables, the strongest-coefficient convention — is shared unchanged with eType II R16, now applied per TRP.

How many TRPs cooperate ($N_{TRP} \in \{1,2,3,4\}$) and how many beams each contributes are both configurable; a `mode1` reports the inter-TRP phase explicitly, while `mode2` skips it and lets the per-coefficient phases alone carry the alignment. With $N_{TRP}=1$ the codebook degenerates exactly to plain R16 eType II — the same kind of clean sanity-check seen elsewhere in this family. A port-selection sibling (§5.2.2.2.9) swaps each TRP's DFT beam search for freely selected ports, mirroring R17's relationship to R16.

The cost of coordinating $N$ sites is, unsurprisingly, roughly $N$ times an eType II report, plus a small TRP-selection bitmap and the inter-TRP phase offsets. That is the honest price of multi-site coherence: CJT buys the array-gain and interference-cancellation benefits of a much larger distributed antenna, at overhead that grows roughly linearly with the number of cooperating sites.

howItWorks.title: UE measures the channel from every cooperating TRP
howItWorks.body: The phone estimates the channel separately from each transmission point's CSI-RS (channel state information reference signal) resource, stacked along the antenna-port axis with each TRP occupying its own block of ports.

howItWorks.title: UE selects beams independently, per TRP
howItWorks.body: For each TRP, the same energy-capture group-and-beam search used by eType II R16 picks that TRP's own L beams from its own orthogonal group — different sites can favor different directions.

howItWorks.title: UE finds the shared delay taps and the inter-TRP phase offsets
howItWorks.body: A common set of delay taps is chosen across all TRPs and layers (as in R16), and, in mode1, a frequency-dependent phase offset per non-reference TRP is estimated to align that site's signal with the reference TRP.

howItWorks.title: UE quantizes amplitude, phase, and the coefficient bitmap across all TRPs
howItWorks.body: The strongest coefficient across the combined beam set (spanning every TRP) becomes the fixed reference; the rest are quantized with R16's amplitude/phase tables and pruned to the coefficient budget.

howItWorks.title: Report travels over the uplink
howItWorks.body: The per-TRP beam indices, the TRP-selection bitmap, the shared delay taps, the inter-TRP phase offsets, and the quantized coefficients form the PMI (precoding matrix indicator).

howItWorks.title: gNB reconstructs a stacked precoder and every TRP transmits coherently
howItWorks.body: The network rebuilds one precoder block per TRP, applies the reported inter-TRP phase alignment, and every cooperating site transmits its block so the signals combine constructively at the phone.

whatIsReported.plain: Each TRP's own orthogonal beam group.
whatIsReported.detail: One rotation indicator per cooperating TRP, analogous to R16's i11 but repeated per site since each TRP has its own angular view of the phone.
One combinatorial beam-combination index per TRP, sized to that TRP's configured beam count L_j.

whatIsReported.plain: The shared delay taps used by every TRP, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: A single set of delay taps common to all cooperating TRPs and used identically to R16's frequency compression.

whatIsReported.plain: A bitmap over the combined beam set (all TRPs) and delay taps, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: R16's bitmap generalized to span every TRP's beams, not just one site's.

whatIsReported.plain: Which coefficient, across all TRPs, is the strongest, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: Same dual-mode indicator as R16, now indexed over the concatenated multi-TRP beam axis.

whatIsReported.plain: The relative phase/timing offset of each non-reference TRP.
whatIsReported.detail: In mode1, one offset per cooperating TRP beyond the first (reference) TRP, entering the reconstruction as a frequency-dependent phase ramp that aligns that site's contribution; not reported at all in mode2.

whatIsReported.plain: The per-polarization reference amplitude, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: Same mechanism as R16, applied across the combined multi-TRP coefficient set.

parametersExplained.plain: How many transmission points (1-4) cooperate to serve the phone.
parametersExplained.guidance: n_trp=1 reduces exactly to eType II R16 — start there to see the baseline before adding cooperating sites.

parametersExplained.plain: The per-TRP beam-count preset (which TRPs get how many beams).
parametersExplained.guidance: Symmetric combinations (equal beams per TRP) are a reasonable default; asymmetric ones let stronger TRPs carry more beams.

parametersExplained.plain: Whether the CJT codebook uses DFT beams (regular) or freely selected ports (port-selection) per TRP.
parametersExplained.guidance: Pick port-selection when each TRP's CSI-RS is already beamformed, mirroring the R16-vs-R17 choice for a single site.

strengths: Unlocks distributed-array gains — coherent combining across multiple physically separated sites.
Reuses R16's proven per-beam compression and quantization machinery almost unchanged, per TRP.
Degenerates exactly to plain eType II R16 at n_trp=1, giving a clean baseline comparison.
The port-selection sibling extends the same TRP-cooperation idea to reciprocity-based deployments.
limitations: Overhead scales roughly linearly with the number of cooperating TRPs — the most expensive report family in this catalog.
Requires all TRPs to be tightly synchronized and share timing/phase references for coherent combining to actually work in practice.
Subset restriction is only supported at the coarse '00'/'11' codepoints per resource, not the full amplitude-cap granularity of plain R16.
TRP resource selection (which subset of the configured TRPs actually cooperate) is a bitmap layered on top of an already large report.
whenToUse: Use CJT to model multi-site cooperative deployments (distributed MIMO / cell-free-style clusters) and to see how feedback overhead scales as more transmission points join the cooperating set.

mathHighlight.caption: Every non-reference TRP's contribution carries an extra frequency-dependent phase ramp that aligns it with the reference TRP for coherent combining.
mathHighlight.latex: \text{TRP }j:\quad e^{\,j2\pi t\psi_j/N_3} \sum_i v_{m_{1,j}^{(i)},m_{2,j}^{(i)}}\, p^{(1)}_{l,\cdot}\, \sum_f y_{t,l}^{(f)}\, p^{(2)}_{l,i,f,j}\, \varphi_{l,i,f,j}
